coredata.dc:title: Exploring carbon quantum dots as an aqueous electrolyte for energy storage devices
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Satendra
Goswami, Manoj
Singh, Netrapal
Sathish, N.
Reddy, M.V.
Kumar, Surender
coredata.dc:description: The facile accessibility of electroactive species to the surface of graphene-based supercapacitors is crucial. Here, we report the potential of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as an electrolyte for graphene-based supercapacitors. The aqueous CQDs electrolyte properties are explored and compared with other commonly used aqueous electrolytes. A gravimetric capacitance of 155 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g is achieved with an aqueous 25 mM CQDs electrolyte having 0.43 S/cm ionic conductivity. The energy density of as-fabricated device is calculated to be ~55 Wh/kg at a power density of 3200 W/kg with a 1.6 V potential window (PW). Also, our approach comprises a solution-based flexible and free-standing CQDs-polymer gel (f-CPG) electrolyte film formation. The f-CPG film reveals an ionic conductivity of 0.48 S/cm and a high dielectric constant (972) with low dielectric loss (~12) at 2 MHz frequency at room temperature. The f-CPG electrolyte film exhibits a considerable electrochemical performance with gravimetric capacitance, energy, and power densities of 140 F/g, 50 Wh/kg, and 8534 W/kg at 2 A/g, respectively. The CQDs electrolyte could be an excellent choice for energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Electrolyte
Potential window
Ionic conductivity
Flexible CQDs film
Graphene supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis protocol of CQDs from citric acid
Studies of the f-CPG electrolyte film
Electrochemical study of symmetric SSS device fabricated with f-CPG film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CQDs synthesis protocol from CA by pyrolysis method.
Schematics of the SSS device fabrication process and corresponding photographs of the SSS device.
Various CQDs characterizations: (a) UV–Vis spectrum: bandgap calculation through Tauc plot shown in the inset, (b) FT-IR spectra, and (c) Raman spectra.
CQD's TEM results: (a) TEM (HRTEM in the inset), (b) particle distribution, and (c) SAED pattern. High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (d), O 1s (e), and complete survey (f).
Studies of (a) PW of aqueous 25 mM CQDs through CV and (b) ionic conductivity through EIS of chosen commonly used electrolytes along with CQDs electrolyte.

Electrochemical results of symmetric SSS device made with 25 mM CQDs electrolyte: (a) CV profiles at different scan rates, (b) the GCD profiles at current densities of 1, 2, and 5 A/g, and (c) Rate capability test of GNP electrode under aqueous 25 mM CQDs electrolyte at various current rates. Electrochemical results of chosen commonly used electrolytes under two-electrodes test: (d) CV curves at 50 mV/s scan rate, (e) zoomed CV curves beyond 1.2 V, and (f) GCD profiles at 3 A/g current density. The 25 mM CQDs electrolyte-based symmetric SSS device results: (g) CV curves at different scan rates, (h) GCD profile at 0.1 A/g current density (gloving green LED in the inset), and (i) fitted EIS profile (equivalent circuit model is shown in the inset, R2: charge-transfer resistance). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Digital images of prepared CPG gel electrolyte (a, b), CPG electrolyte cast on a glass slide (c), and f-CPG film under UV exposure (d). The f-CPG electrolyte study: actual PW (e), EIS plot for ionic conductivity measurement of CMC-CQD slurry (f), log-log plot of dielectric constant (g), and dielectric loss (h) w.r.t frequency, and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curve (i) of f-CPG film.

Electrochemical results of symmetric SSS device fabricated from f-CPG electrolyte film separator: (a) CV profiles, (b) GCD profiles, (c) before and after cycle EIS, (d) Coulombic efficiency and capacitance retention plots at 6 A/g current density, (e) leakage current test, and (f) OCP stability test.
Comparison table of the PW and ionic conductivity.
Comparison of electrochemical results of chosen electrolytes.

head.ce:title: Exploring carbon quantum dots as an aqueous electrolyte for energy storage devices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad 201002, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad - 201002, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR - Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute (AMPRI), Bhopal 462026, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR - Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute (AMPRI)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CSIR - Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute (AMPRI), Bhopal - 462026, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nouveau Monde Graphite (NMG), Saint-Michel-des-Saints, Québec J0K 3B0, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nouveau Monde Graphite (NMG), Saint-Michel-des- Saints, Québec, J0K 3B0, Canada

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad 201002, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The facile accessibility of electroactive species to the surface of graphene-based supercapacitors is crucial. Here, we report the potential of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as an electrolyte for graphene-based supercapacitors. The aqueous CQDs electrolyte properties are explored and compared with other commonly used aqueous electrolytes. A gravimetric capacitance of 155 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g is achieved with an aqueous 25 mM CQDs electrolyte having 0.43 S/cm ionic conductivity. The energy density of as-fabricated device is calculated to be ~55 Wh/kg at a power density of 3200 W/kg with a 1.6 V potential window (PW). Also, our approach comprises a solution-based flexible and free-standing CQDs-polymer gel (f-CPG) electrolyte film formation. The f-CPG film reveals an ionic conductivity of 0.48 S/cm and a high dielectric constant (972) with low dielectric loss (~12) at 2 MHz frequency at room temperature. The f-CPG electrolyte film exhibits a considerable electrochemical performance with gravimetric capacitance, energy, and power densities of 140 F/g, 50 Wh/kg, and 8534 W/kg at 2 A/g, respectively. The CQDs electrolyte could be an excellent choice for energy storage devices.

The 25 mM aqueous CQD electrolyte shows a significant potential window of 1.6 V, high ionic conductivity of 0.43, and 0.48 S/cm (in f-CPG film) at room temperature. The fabricated device shows 155 F/g specific capacitance at 1 A/g current density with a 1.6 V potential window. A high energy and power density of 55 Wh/kg and 3200 W/kg is calculated with 72 % capacitance retention after 1000 life cycles at a current density of 6 F/g. The developed f-CPG separator film also shows 140 F/g specific capacitance at a current density of 2 A/g.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The increased demand for environmentally friendly and high-performing electrochemical energy storage devices (EESDs) for consumer electronics and integrated on-chip technologies has accelerated advancement in the energy sector [1–4]. Electrolytes are essential components of supercapacitors that either generate double-layer in electrical double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) or activate the Faradaic reactions of pseudocapacitors [5]. Electrolytes are classified into three categories as liquid, solid, and polymer gel (PG) electrolytes, according to their physical state. Liquid electrolytes have suitable ionic conductivity (10−3–10−2 S/cm) [6–8] but are often igneous and require rigid packing. Solid electrolytes provide safe functioning, but their energy storage performance is limited due to their depleted ionic conductivity (10−8–10−5 S/cm) [9]. PG electrolytes have the best recipe for high ionic conductivity and excellent operational safety [7]. To fabricate a high-performing PG electrolyte, selecting the type of base polymer, solvent, and electrolyte salt is also important.

As we know, carbon dots (CDs) are a comprehensive term for various nanosized carbon materials. In a broad sense, all nanosized materials composed mainly of carbon can be called CDs [10]. CDs always possess at least one dimension <10 nm in size and fluorescence as their instinct properties. The structure of CDs consists of sp2/sp3 carbon and oxygen/nitrogen-based groups or polymeric aggregations. CDs mainly include graphene quantum dots (GQDs), CQDs, carbon nanodots (CNDs), and polymer dots (PDs) [11]. GQDs possess one or a few layers of graphene and connected chemical groups on the edges. They are anisotropic with lateral dimensions larger than their height. CNDs are always spherical, and they are divided into carbon nanoparticles, which do not have a crystal lattice, while CQDs, which have an obvious crystal lattice. Among various graphene-based materials, GQDs hold novel optoelectronic properties [12,13]. So far, GQDs are exploited for various optoelectronic and energy-related applications [14]. For the first time, Zhang et al. [15] reported GQDs as a salt that can be used for electrolyte purposes in energy-related applications. Ajayan et al. explored graphene oxide (GO) as an electrolyte and separator simultaneously [16]. The oxygen-bearing carbonyl groups (-OH, -C=O, and -COOH) is decorated on GO surfaces that result in high ionic conductivity (~0.029 S/cm) due to a substantial amount of H3O+ ions [17,18]. However, functional groups that introduce sp3 hybridized carbon atoms damage the sp2 structure of graphene flakes, making GO an electrical insulator. Likewise, CQDs have a sufficient number of oxygen-bearing carbonyl groups that make them water dispersible and can be considered oxidative debris [19,20]. As a result, CQDs can also be able to generate H3O+ ions due to weak acidic functional groups present on their surface and hence can be used as solution-type and PG electrolytes for EESDs.

Carbon-based electrode nanomaterials have long been the preferred electrode material for fabricating low-cost, high-efficiency supercapacitors [21,22]; graphene, in particular, has outperformed compared to other carbon-based electrode materials [23]. Supercapacitors have been gaining popularity because of their extraordinary power density, quick charge/discharge rate, outstanding cycle longevity, and safety [24,25]. It has high inherent capacitance (~21 μF cm−2) [26] as well as excellent mechanical flexibility and strength (~1 TPa), and good electrical conductivity (250,000 cm2/V-s) [27,28].

A 25 mM CQDs are investigated as aqueous and f-CPG electrolytes for EDLC in this paper. Furthermore, CQDs' ionic conductivity (or ion-donating capacity) might be significantly increased by merely neutralizing the weak acidic oxygen-bearing functional groups. The aqueous CQD electrolyte shows a PW of 1.6 V, which is utilized in this study. The capacitance of the EDLC was considerably increased when neutralized CQDs are used as f-CPG electrolytes. We constructed graphene-based solid-state supercapacitor (SSS) devices with CQDs as solid-and PG-type electrolytes. The real SSS device was fabricated with solid-type CQDs electrolyte-powered green LED for ~50 s after charging for 225 s up to a PW of 1.6 V. An f-CPG electrolyte separator film was made and successfully used in EDLC. Our observations might yield insights into using CQDs as an electrolyte, constructing graphene-based SSS devices, and broadening the range of CQDs applications.
Supplementary information includes a description of the precursor materials and data tables.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis protocol of CQDs from citric acid
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were synthesized by the pyrolysis method. Briefly, a 5 g of citric acid (CA) was incompletely heated at 200 °C for 25 min in a vacuum furnace at 1 mbar chamber pressure (Ar-100 sccm) [29]. After natural cooling, the obtained yellowish sticky semi-solid was neutralized by adding 6 M KOH solution dropwise. The synthesis protocol is shown in Fig. 1 . In short, when heated to its melting point, citric acid decomposes, and the hydronium ion generated functions as a catalyst in subsequent decomposition reactions. Indeed, the polymerization and condensation of the products resulted in a soluble polymer. Aldol condensation and cycloaddition are used to aromatize and create aromatic clusters. Burst nucleation occurs when the concentration of aromatic clusters hits a threshold supersaturation point, and CQDs are formed.

The electrodes were made by mixing 85 wt% of the graphene nanoplatelets (GNPs) and 15 wt% of polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) powders. Initially, the mixture was ground properly, and then N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) was added dropwise as a solvent. The slurry was then coated on copper foil by brush-coating method and dried at 60 °C overnight in the oven. The copper foil was cleaned properly before use through ethanol, acetone, and de-ionized (DI) water subsequently to remove surface impurities. Particularly, carbon-based conductive additives are used during electrode preparation, exhibiting a large specific surface area and good electrical conductivity. The main role of conductive additives is to generate a percolating network for electron transport in the electrode layer, significantly improving electrode conductivity. On the other hand, graphene-based carbon nanomaterials already have a large specific surface area. Carbon black has a specific surface area of <100 m2/g [30], while used GNPs have nearly 1000 m2/g. Here, GNPs are carbon-based nanomaterial with a high specific surface area and electrical conductivity that can also be used as a conductive agent.

The f-CPG electrolyte film was prepared by adding carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) in CQDs suspension in the ratio of 1:30 w/v%, 1 g CMC powder was added to the 50 mL beaker containing 30 mL of 25 mM CQDs suspension and left overnight to dissolve without any external means. Finally, the slurry was stirred vigorously for homogeneous mixing of CMC into CQDs suspension.

For comparative study, SSSs was fabricated by placing the Whatman filter paper as a separator soaked in corresponding electrolytes. The SSS cell consists of the f-CPG electrolyte film sandwiched between two GNPs electrodes, as shown in Fig. 2 . The f-CPG film behaves as a separator as well as an electrolyte.

Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy was used to study the optical properties of as-prepared CQDs and f-CPG film. Fig. 3a shows the UV–Vis spectrum of as-prepared CQDs suspension, which yielded a typical n-π* transition peak around 354 nm. The n-π* transition is the result of the appearance of oxygenated functional groups like CO, CO, and epoxy at the edge of CQDs. The photograph in the inset shows the fluorescence and is compared with DI water and diluted CQDs suspension. The colour variation in the fluorescence images is noted due to the agglomeration tendency of CQDs suspension at a concentration >1 mg/mL [31,32]. The direct bandgap of CQDs was calculated from the Tauc equation, which was 4.3 eV, as shown in the inset. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy also studied the synthesized CQDs to validate the UV–Vis results, as shown in Fig. 3b. The FT-IR spectroscopy shows the stretching of CO at 1640 cm−1, stretching of CC at 1565 cm−1, stretching of double peaks of CO at 1275 cm−1 and 1093 cm−1, aromatic CC at 2120 cm−1, and presence of OH at 3340 cm−1 [33]. From the FT-IR spectrum, it is very clear that the synthesized CQDs were nicely decorated with many oxygen-containing groups like C-O-C (1380 cm−1), CO, CO, and O-H/COOH.

The Raman spectroscopy of CQDs suspension in the spectral range 1000–2000 cm−1 is shown in Fig. 3c. The spectrum shows a typical graphitic feature, including the G-band (~1569 cm−1), which results from the in-plane CC distortions and D-band (~1357 cm−1) actuated by symmetry contravention at edges and defects [34]. The observed spectrum was independent of the measurement position means synthesized CQDs yielded features of extended graphene structures. Due to the dispersive nature of second-order double-resonance of Raman scattering, D-band activity can be attributed to quantum confinement phenomena. The D-mode phenomena begin with optical excitation and subsequently two intervalley scattering experiences for the electron (or hole) within the graphene Brillouin zone, each involving either a D phonon [35]. While the G-mode frequency is dependent on CQDs size, which gives quantitative information. The fact is that the G-mode arises from a first-order scattering mechanism that is unaffected by quantum confinement. As an alternative, G-mode frequency shifted toward the larger strain side due to the smaller size of CQDs of continuous sp2 conjugated lattices. The I
D /I
G ratio in the graphene-based materials gives quantitative information on the defect density (n
D ) and inter-defect spacings (L
D ). The inter-defect spacing of synthesized CQDs varied from 6 to 16 nm (calculated from Eq. (1): Tuinstra-Koenig relation), while the defect density was 2.7 × 1011 cm−2 (calculated from Eq. (2)) [36].
(1)
L D 2
nm 2
=
1.8 ± 0.5
×
10
− 9
λ L 4
I D
I G
− 1
(2)
n D
cm
− 2
=
1.8 ± 0.5
×
10 22
λ L 4
I D
I G
where, λ
L , I
D , and I
G are the laser source wavelength (532 nm), the intensity of D-mode, and the intensity of G-mode, respectively.

Fig. 4a shows the transmission electron microscopes (TEM) result of as-prepared CQDs, and the high resolution-TEM image (in the inset) shows the localized lattice fringes of CQDs with in-plane fringes spacing of 0.21 nm, which is attributed to (1120) [37]. The size of as-prepared CQDs ranged from 3 to 7 nm (diameter), whose distribution is shown in Fig. 4b that was uniformly dispersed. Fig. 4c depicts the CQD's selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern, which reveals the CQD's six-fold symmetry of the crystalline structure. The composition of functional groups in as-prepared CQDs was determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS survey profiles (Fig. 4d–f) of as-prepared CQDs exhibit a graphitic C 1s peak at 284.8 eV and an O 1s peak at 532.8 eV. The existence of oxygen-rich groups, including CO (286.2 eV), CO (287.4 eV), and O-C=O (288.8 eV), was further validated by the high-resolution C 1s profile of the CQDs (Fig. 4d), which is commensurate with the respective FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3b). The percentage contribution of CC, CO, CO, and O-C=O functional groups are 72, 20.5, 5.5, and 2 %, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s is shown in Fig. 4e, which shows major oxygenated functional groups at their respective positions with a C/O atomic ratio of 0.23. The XPS survey in Fig. 4f confirms no impurity peaks.

Raman spectroscopy validated the layer number in the pristine GNPs powder. GNP's signature is depicted in Fig. S1a, where the I
2D /I
G ratio is 0.46. The G-band (1584 cm−1) is associated with the E2g in-plane phonon mode around the Brillouin zone centre, D-band (1352 cm−1) is linked with the breathing mode of sp2 atoms, and the 2D-band (2724 cm−1) is associated with second-order phonons. A brief shoulder is deconvoluted from the 2D band at 2684 cm−1 and can be accredited to the graphene 2D1A band. The existence of the 2D band and the placement of the G-band suggest that the graphene sheets used have multi-layer (>10 layers) [38–40]. For the SSS device fabrication, multilayer graphene (or GNPs) was used, whose TEM result is shown in Fig. S1b. The High-resolution-TEM result reveals the presence of (002) lattice with a d-spacing of 0.36 nm (in the inset), which is slightly more than that of bulk graphite (0.336 nm).

To see the possibilities of prepared 25 mM CQDs as an aqueous and PG electrolyte, first electrochemical activities were performed to see the working PW through cyclic voltammetry and ionic conductivity through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) at room temperature. Fig. 5a shows the forward scan of cyclic voltammetry (CV), which depicts the actual PW of aqueous 25 mM CQDs electrolyte. The actual PW of the aqueous 25 mM CQDs electrolyte is nearly 1.6 V, while extended PW is 0.9 V. The PWs of all chosen commonly used aqueous electrolytes are shown in Table 1 . The CVs for PW identification of chosen commonly used aqueous electrolytes (1 M Na2SO4, 1 M NaOH, 2 M Li2SO4, 4 M NaClO4, and 6 M KOH) are shown in Fig. S2a–e. Due to the thermodynamical decomposition of water, an aqueous electrolyte cannot achieve a PW beyond 1.8 V. Whereas a saturated aqueous solution of sodium perchlorate with a PW of 3.2 V in terms of CV [41]. Considering the above study, we can also consider aqueous CQDs electrolytes of 2.5 V (including an extended PW of 0.9 V) comparable with water-in-salt electrolytes. Fig. 5b shows the EIS spectra which are used to find the ionic conductivity (σ) of all aqueous electrolytes and calculated from Eq. (3) [42]:
(3)
σ =
L
R 1
· A
where L is the length of the platinum electrode, A is the exposed area, and R
1 is the equivalent series resistance (ESR) after fitting.

The highest ionic conductivity was found for 4 M NaClO4 (1.37 S/cm), while the lowest for 1 M Na2SO4 (0.26 S/cm) among chosen commonly used aqueous electrolytes. The low ionic conductivity of 1 M Na2SO4 was due to the slow ionic diffusion (less steep, in Fig. 5b). The ionic conductivity of 25 mM CQDs (0.43 S/cm) is more than that of 1 M Na2SO4, resulting from better ionic diffusion [43–46]. The low ionic conductivity of 25 mM CQDs electrolytes results from the partial ionization of different oxygenated functional groups. All the EIS data was fitted with a standard equivalent circuit model, as shown in Fig. S2f. From the above results, it can be concluded here that 25 mM CQDs can be used effectively as an aqueous electrolyte for energy storage.

After exploring the ionic conductivity and actual PW, as-prepared 25 mM CQDs are used as an aqueous electrolyte to fabricate the SSS with GNPs electrodes. Fig. 6 shows extensive electrochemical studies of the 25 mM CQDs as an electrolyte. Fig. 6a shows a typical capacitive behaviour (nearly rectangular without redox peaks) of GNP electrodes under CV study when 25 mM CQDs are used as solid-state electrolytes. The CVs at scan rates of 50, 100, 300, and 500 mV/s are typically following SSS behaviour up to a PW of 1.6 V. This indicates the electrochemical double-layer formation in GNPs electrodes in which the way of energy storage is centred on the separation of charges by adsorption-desorption on its surface. The polarization arising from the internal resistance of the electrode material can reduce the utilization of maximum PW [47]. Hence, we selected a full PW of 1.6 V to see the potential working of CQDs electrolyte and the polarization from the internal resistance of the electrode material. The actual specific capacitance of the symmetric device (Cdev) was calculated from the galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curve, as shown in Fig. 6b, following Eq. (4):
(4)
C dev
= 4
i · ∆ t
∆ V
F / g
where i correspond to current density (A/g), Δt stands for discharge time (s), and ΔV is the actual PW (V) [1]. The energy (Edev) and power (Pdev) densities were obtained from the following Eqs. (5) and (6):
(5)
E dev
=
1 7.2
·
C dev
· ∆
V 2
Wh / kg
(6)
P dev
=
E dev
× 3600
∆ t
W / kg

The specific capacitances of the symmetric SSS device were recorded 155, 70, and 40 F/g at 1, 2, and 5 A/g current densities, respectively, for 25 mM CQDs electrolyte under a full PW scale of 1.6 V. The energy density of SSS was calculated to be ~55 Wh/kg at a power density of 3200 W/kg. The electrochemical results of 25 mM CQDs electrolyte are compared in table S1, showing considerable performance. Subsequently, the GNP electrodes are subjected to numerous current densities, and rate capability behaviour is investigated under an aqueous 25 mM CQDs electrolyte. Nominal specific capacitance values are obtained with increasing current densities from 0.5 to 5 A/g, shown in Fig. 6c. Again, a nominal specific capacitance (90.5 %) is achieved at 0.5 A/g after going from 0.5 to 5 A/g and to coming to 0.5 A/g, which is quite encouraging. Fig. S3a shows the EIS profile of the device before (black) and after (red) 1000 life cycle tests. The typical EIS profile shows a slight increment in the electrode resistance from 0.82 to 1.85 Ω after 1000 cycles. The linear region of the EIS profile was linearly fitted at lower frequencies range for the determination of ionic diffusion capability with the help of slope. The slope was slightly reduced from 1.37 to 1.18, which depicts slow ionic diffusion after 1000 cycles of charge-discharge due to degradation of electroactive species. Fig. S3b shows a ~72 % capacity retention and ~100 % Coulombic efficiency after 1000 cycles at a current density of 6 A/g.

After the preliminary test of aqueous 25 mM CQDs as an electrolyte of working PW of 1.6 V, the other chosen commonly used electrolytes were also studied under the two-electrodes system in comparison with CQDs electrolyte. The CVs are typically rectangular at a 50 mV/s scan rate for all the interested electrolytes under a PW of 1.6 V, as shown in Fig. 6d. A 5-fold current density was achieved for 25 mM CQDs electrolytes as compared to other commonly used aqueous electrolytes under a PW of 1.6 V. To make a clear pathway to understand the electrolyte working beyond the thermodynamic potential of water, Fig. 6e shows the zoomed CV curves beyond 1.2 V. The chosen commonly used electrolytes cannot provide sufficient current density beyond 1.2 V, and some are losing their current density due to the thermodynamic potential of water. The GCD profiles at 3 A/g current density are shown in Fig. 6f for the two-electrodes system of all the chosen commonly used aqueous electrolytes. The GCD profile under each electrolyte is typically triangular and delivers the highest specific capacitance (65.37 F/g) for 25 mM CQDs electrolyte. The EIS spectra of all the selected electrolytes are shown in Fig. S3c in the frequency range of 0.1–100 kHz with 5 mV perturbation. The electrochemical parameters obtained from GCD and EIS studies under the two-electrodes system and at a PW of 1.6 V are listed in Table 2 . For commonly used electrolytes, a high Cdev is recorded for 1 M Na2SO4 even though the Cdev for 25 mM CQDs electrolyte is the highest among all (65.37 F/g at 3 A/g). On the contrary, the ionic diffusion is fast for 25 mM CQDs electrolyte (slope: 1.37) compared to all the chosen commonly used aqueous electrolytes. Additionally, the 25 mM CQDs electrolyte is far better than the rest of the chosen commonly used aqueous electrolytes with a high PW of 1.6 V. The good electrochemical performance of 25 mM CQDs electrolyte may result from fast ionic diffusion that motivated us to fabricate an actual device.

After going through the mode of preliminary electrochemical studies and comparison with chosen commonly used aqueous electrolytes, the real SSS device was fabricated with 25 mM CQDs electrolyte and GNPs electrodes. The three sets of pairs of GNPs electrodes are connected in series. Fig. 6g shows the CVs curves at 50, 100, and 200 mV/s scan rates that typically preserve the behaviour of a capacitor under a PW of 1.6 V. The GCD profile at 0.1 A/g current density in Fig. 6h shows the actual charge-discharge profile with a collective Cdev of 11.24 F/g; the working device is shown in the inset. A slight polarization occurred while utilizing a PW of 1.6 V. It can be reduced by making an asymmetric device which has a higher work function difference between electrodes. Specifically, we are utilizing the ‘maximum charging voltage’ of the device with our electrolyte to see the stability of the device at the expense of the life cycle. As compared to the ESR value (0.82 Ω) of the three-electrode configuration test (Fig. S3), the device resistance (1.10 Ω) is slightly large. On the other hand, electrode material quality, parallel and series stacking of electrodes, device packaging, connecting wires, etc., increase the device resistance and lower the current density. These might be the major factors affecting the device's charge-discharge profile, which needs to be optimized. The 25 mM CQDs electrolyte-based SSS device glows a green LED for ~50 s after 225 s of charging up to a PW of 1.6 V. A 1.10 Ω resistance is observed for fabricated device through EIS measurement, as shown in Fig. 6i.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies of the f-CPG electrolyte film
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the flexible CQDs solid-state separator film, a fixed amount of CMC (1 g) was mixed in an aqueous solution of 25 mM CQDs (30 mL). This mixture had a colour change from yellow to light brown due to the absorption of 25 mM CQDs in the 230–700 nm wavelength range [14]. After homogenizing the CQDs and CMC mixture, the quantitative examination was done by flip test, in Fig. 7a (immediate) and Fig. 7b (after 1 h). A thin film separator was prepared on a glass slide (Fig. 7c) and then gently peeled off from the glass slide, and fluorescence was tested from the UV cabinet. The f-CPG film of aqueous 25 mM CQDs electrolyte is shown in Fig. 7d under 365 nm UV exposure. The f-CPG film shows the approximate actual PW (1.6 V, shown in Fig. 7e) compared to the aqueous CQDs electrolyte discussed above. The small quantity of CMC polymer (1 g in 30 mL of 25 mM CQDs) does not affect the actual PW. The f-CPG film shows an ESR of 13.5 Ω (Fig. 7f), which is notably higher than aqueous CQDs electrolyte (0.82 Ω) due to the addition of CMC biocompatible polymer matrix. Instead, the ionic conductivity of f-CPG film (0.48 S/cm) is slightly more and comparable with aqueous CQDs electrolyte; at higher frequencies, the diameter of the semicircle increases, which depicts the low interface resistance between the f-CPG electrolyte and the platinum electrodes. The ionic conductivity of PG based-electrolytes is compared with CMC-CQDs polymer gel electrolytes, listed in Table S2.

A large dielectric constant along with low dielectric loss is looked for in a capacitor which can be achieved by introducing a polymer [48]. Fig. 7g shows the relative dielectric constant of the f-CPG film under frequency variation. At room temperature, the dielectric characteristics of the f-CPG film are recorded in the frequency range of 20 Hz–2 MHz. The dielectric constant of the f-CPG film is decreasing with frequency due to the collective effects of dipoles and ionic, electronic, and space charge polarization [49]. The prepared f-CPGs film shows a high dielectric constant (~972) and low dielectric loss (~12) at a 2 MHz frequency. The dielectric constant (Ɛ
r ) was calculated using the below Eq. (7) [50]:
(7)
Ɛ r
=
t ·
C p
Ɛ 0
· A
where t is the thickness of f-CPG film under test, C
P is the parallel plate capacitance, Ɛ
0 is the permittivity of free space, and A is the f-CPG film area under test. The dielectric loss is relatively low in the low-frequency range and exponentially increases after 100 kHz, as shown in Fig. 7h.

The TGA of the f-CPG film was performed up to 400 °C with a temperature ramp of 10 °C per minute under a nitrogen atmosphere, as shown in Fig. 7i. A two-stage weight loss is observed in prepared f-CPG separator film. The first weight loss of ~58 % in the temperature range of 30 to 200 °C is observed due to the moisture content of aqueous 25 mM CQDs suspension present in the f-CPG film. While the second loss of ~5 % is due to the degradation of CMC polymer in the temperature range of 250 to 300 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical study of symmetric SSS device fabricated with f-CPG film
ce:section.ce:para: An in-depth electrochemical analysis of the fabricated symmetric SSS device was performed at room temperature. At scan rates of 50, 100, 300, and 500 mV/s, CV curves exhibited a typical semi-rectangular shape (Fig. 8a) up to a PW of 1.6 V. The EDLC behaviour of the GNPs electrode is preserved under f-CPG separator film with fast ion transport. The GCD profile shows a typical triangular curve at current densities (2, 3, and 4 A/g) up to a PW of 1.6 V, as shown in Fig. 8b. The GCD profile depicts the ideal charge balance and non-redox behaviour curve between f-CPG film and GNPs symmetric electrodes. The Cdev of 140 F/g at 2 A/g is achieved for symmetric SSS devices. The energy density of f-CPG was calculated to be ~50 Wh/kg at a power density of 8534 W/kg. The Nyquist plot of the symmetric SSS device is shown in Fig. 8c, before and after 1000 life cycles. A small semicircle shows the parallel connection between EDLC and the charge transfer resistance (R2) somewhere at the electrode/electrolyte junction at high frequencies, with a series resistance (R1) representing the ESR. The ESR is increased from 0.84 to 5.46 Ω after 1000 cycles. The low R2 (2.22 Ω) value reveals the better ion transport diffusion while it was increased to 15.30 Ω after 1000 cycles due to degradation of f-CPG film. An intermediate frequency range (1–100 Hz) correlates to a transition zone in which the migration of CQD electrolyte ions into the GNP electrodes may be observed. At very low frequencies (0.1–1 Hz), CQDs electrolyte ions move deeper into the porous GNP electrodes (steeper), rapidly increasing the imaginary component of the impedance. The symmetric SSS device also shows a ~99 % Coulombic efficiency after 1000 cycles, while specific capacitance was retained up to ~73 % at 6 A/g (Fig. 8d).

By holding the symmetric SSS device at a fixed open-circuit voltage (OCP) (8 mV), the leakage current of the as-prepared symmetric SSS device was monitored, as shown in Fig. 8e. A linear drop in leakage current is observed, which gradually stabilizes, a clear indication of a solid-state device. The leakage current is 63 μA, lower than formerly reported for all-solid-state devices using polymer gel electrolytes. Miserable leakage current may be attributed to scarcer shuttle reactions generated by contaminants in the electrode materials [51,52]. Fig. 8f depicts the device's self-discharge behaviours under an OCP state. After an hour of self-discharge, the symmetric SSS device retains 87.7 % of its initial charge potential.

Even though the SSS device manufacturing technique has yet to be refined, the symmetric SSS device has shown good flexibility and electrochemical performance of the f-CPG film separator. Many factors, including the thickness of the f-CPG film separator, the concentration of CQDs in the polymer gel, and the current collector, can be optimized in the future to achieve better electrochemical performances of CQDs electrolyte-based SSSs.

A high ionic conductivity 25 mM CQDs electrolyte is prepared for graphene-based SSSs applications, which is an important step toward all-carbon energy storage devices. The presented CQDs electrolytes were prepared by a simple one-step approach. We demonstrated that 25 mM CQDs electrolyte reveals high ionic conductivity of 0.43 (in suspension) and 0.48 S/cm (in f-CPG) at room temperature. The fabricated SSS device exhibit a maximum PW of 1.6 V with a specific capacitance of 155 F/g at 1 A/g for aqueous 25 mM CQDs electrolyte. A high energy density of 55 Wh/kg is obtained at a power density of 3200 W/kg. The as-fabricated device shows a 72 % capacitance retention after 1000 life cycles at a current density of 6 A/g. Additionally, f-CPG electrolyte film was also developed, which has a low leakage current and nearly stable OCP (self-discharge). The specific capacitance for f-CPG electrolyte film was found 140 F/g at 2 A/g with a 1.6 V working PW. The energy density of f-CPG was calculated to be ~50 Wh/kg at a power density of 8534 W/kg. Due to their high ionic conductivity and ion-donating capacity, the solution- and f-CPG electrolyte film might be employed as electrolytes for energy storage devices.

Satendra Kumar: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Manoj Goswami: Validation, Data curation. Netrapal Singh: Software, Formal analysis. N. Sathish: Supervision, Validation. M.V. Reddy: Project administration, Resources. Surender Kumar: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Author(s) declare no Conflict of Interest involved in this manuscript titled “Exploring Carbon Quantum Dots as an Aqueous Electrolyte for Energy Storage Devices”. This manuscript is checked and approved by the authors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Author thanks Director CSIR-AMPRI, Bhopal, for extending the characterization facilities. The author acknowledges SERB, New Delhi (File no; EEQ/2018/000068), and the CSIR-NET fellowship (File No: 31/041(0078)/2019-EMR-I) for their support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2022.105522.

Iwakura, Electrochemical characteristics of new electric double layer capacitor with acidic polymer hydrogel electrolyte, J. Power Sources. 159 (2006) 1464–1467. doi:10.1016/j.jpowsour.2005.11.073.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphitic petal electrodes for all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [52] G. Xiong, C. Meng, R.G. Reifenberger, P.P. Irazoqui, T.S. Fisher, Graphitic petal electrodes for all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors, Adv. Energy Mater. 4 (2014) 1–9. doi:10.1002/aenm.201300515.
